Source: 81[395{From Proto-Slavic *pȅpelъ | *pȍpelъ  m. o (c) ‘ashes’, PIE *pepelh₁-o-, cf. p. 390 for the discussion of the root.}];141[469{Reconstructs the PIE root as *?pel- 'to flame up', i.e. without the laryngeal. }];352[752-753{S.v. OLith. pẽlenas 'Asche| Herd' (BSl. Latv. pȩ̀lni 'Asche', OPr. pelanne 'Asche', pelanno 'Herd'). Different etymological connections are considered:}];259[463{PS *popel- and *pepel-. Reflex of PIE *pel- 'burn' with a reduplication.}];598[234{Related to Old Prussian pelanne, Lithuanian pelenaĩ, Latvian pę́lni 'ash' and probably to Latin pollen 'fine flour', pulvis 'dust', Greek πάλη 'dust, fine flour'. Reduplication of the root.}];591[430{PS *po-pel- is a compound of the prefix *po- and root *pel- with a meaning 'substance that remains after burning'.}];593[488{Two possible etymologies are *po-pel- 'subtance that remains after burning' and a relation to Latin pollen 'dust, flour', Greek πάλη and Old Indic pálala- 'poridge, dust, dirt'.}];595[3: 25{Most probably *pel- 'burn' with a reduplication.}];594[{A reduplicated root *pelH- 'dust, flour', found also in Latin pollen, pulvis Old Indic pálala- Greek πάλη, παιπάλη, and in PS *pelv- 'chaff'. A relation to *pel- 'burn' is less probable.}];608[332-333{Reduplicated root *pel- 'burn'.}];636[117-118{Reduplication of *pel- 'burn', related to Old Indic palalam 'a kind of poridge', Old Prussian pelanne 'ash', Lithuanian pelenaĩ 'ash', Latvian pélni 'ash', Old Greek πάλη 'dust', παιπάλη 'fine flour', παλύνο 'strew', Latin pollen 'fine flour', pulvis 'dust', and palea 'chaff'.}];531[445-446{The etymology is not entirely clear. Most probably a reflex of PIE *pel- 'burn' and related to reduplicated Slavic form *popel-.}];589[638{Reduplication of *pel- 'dust, flour' or *pel- 'burn'. The first variant is more probable.}];249[{There are two hypotheses of the etymology of the Slavic *popel-, *pepel-. The first one assumes here a root ?*pel- 'burn' which has, however, very sparse - if any certain - attestation beyond Slavic (cf. LIV: 469). The second one assumes a relationship to various IE lexemes with meanings 'dust', '(fine) flour', 'chaff', 'poridge', etc. (*pelh₁- and/or *(s)pelH-). However, some of these lexemes are not necessarily related to each other (de Vaan 2008:  440, 477, 498). Some of the cited Slavic sources acknowledge two independent PIE roots in this case, while some other only one root with a strong semantic differentiation. From the semantic point of view the assumption of a separate root ?*pel- 'burn' and assigning the Slavic word for 'ash' to this root seems to be most resonable.}];321[258{S.v. Wakhi parg 'зола, пепел', to IE *pel- (Ru. пе-пел, Lith. pelenaĩ).}]

81[242{S.v. Proto-Slavic *kòža f. 'skin, leather' (ESSJa XII 35-36), a derivative of Proto-Slavic *kozà f. 'goat', possibly a borrowing from a Turkic language.}];458[12: 19-21, 35-36{PS *koža is derived from PS *koza 'goat'. Its original meaning was 'goat leather'. PS *koza 'goat' itself has probably a non-IE origin.}]
Comment: For the discussion on the further etymology of PS *koza and its relationship to PIE *ag̑- (cf. Old Indic ajā́) see [ESSJa](src-458): 12: 19-21 and 1: 103-104.
Justification: Lexemes continuing Proto-Slavic *koža 'skin, leather', further derived from Proto-Slavic *koza 'goat' originally in the sense 'goat leather'; further etymology uncertain but probably a Turkic loanword ([Derksen 2008](src-81):242, [ESSJa](src-458) 12:35-36).

Source: 352[714{S.v. OLith. nùgara 'Rücken', the word is not to be separated from Latv. mugura 'Rücken', which is also found as mugara. The sound-correspondence Lith. n ~ Latv. m is irregular, and the origins of the lexemes remain unclear.}];531[429{S.v. Lith. nùgara 'grzbiet, plecy'. No etymology.}]
Justification: No certain etymology outside of East Baltic (cf. [ALEW](src-352) 714, [Smoczyński 2007](src-531):429).

Source: 80[794{S.v. Hitt. šūu-, šūu̯au̯- 'full', which may go back to either *souh₁-(o)u- or *souh₃-(o)u- by internal reconstruction.  There are no certain cognates outside of Anatolian.}]
Justification: No clear etymology outside of Anatolian ([Kloekhorst 2008](src-80):794).

Source: 274[417-423{S.v. *k̑ḗr, k̑r̥d-´ n. 'Herz' (IEW 579f.).}];80[469-471{S.v. Hitt. ker / kard(i)- 'heart| center, core', from PIE *k̑ēr, *k̑rd-i-ós, *k̑rd-´.}];172[II:818{S.v. Ved. hŕ̥d- 'Herz', from IIr. *j́ʰā̆rd- /*j́ʰr̥d-, is not completely lautgesetzlich with PIE *k̑ē̆rd-/*k̑r̥d-. The presence of non-etymological *j́ʰ (> Vedic h) in Proto-Indo-Iranian is presumably due to contamination with the Proto-Indo-Iranian outcome of PIE *g̑ʰórh₁-/g̑ʰr̥h₁- 'guts', cf. cognate set 2672.}];66[644{S.v. καρδία 'heart' < PIE *k̑ēr(d), gen. *k̑rd-(i)os 'heart'.}];79[576-578{S.v. Arm. sirt 'heart', since long derived from PIE *k̑ērd, *k̑r̥d-ós 'heart', etc.}];81[485{S.v. Proto-Slavic *sьrdьce 'heart', with a stem derived from the PIE zero-grade *k̑r̥d-.}];353[448-449{S.v. Lith. širdìs 'heart', from PIE *k̑ērd-, G.sg. *k̑r̥d-ós.}];165[222{S.v. Proto-Germanic *hertōn- 'heart', from a derived n-stem *k̑erd-n-.}];50[223-224{S.v. Proto-Celtic *kridyo- 'heart'.}];54[134-135{S.v. Lat. cor, cordis 'heart', from PIE *k̑ēr, k̑r̥d-os. Latin generalised the oblique (zero-grade) form of the stem *k̑r̥d- > *kord-.}];352[1032-1034{S.v. OLith. širdìs 'Herz, Sinn, Gesinnung' (BSl. cf. Latv. sir̂ds 'Herz| Mut, Zorn', OPr. seyr 'Herz', OCS srьcьce 'Herz', etc.), from PIE *k̑ḗrd-/*k̑r̥d- 'Herz' (cf. Hitt. ker 'Herz' CLuw. zārza 'Herz', Ved. hā́rdi, OAv. zərəd- 'Herz', Gk, κῆρ, 'Herz', καρδία 'Herz| Seele, Geist' Arm. sirt 'Herz', Lat. cor, cordis 'Herz| Geist, Seele', OIr. crude 'Herz', Goth. hairto, etc.).}]
Justification: Anatolian, Hellenic, Armenian, Indo-Iranic, Balto-Slavic, Germanic, Italic, and Celtic lexemes from PIE *k̑erd- 'heart' ([NIL](src-274) 417-423, cf. [Kloekhorst 2008](src-80):469-471, [Beekes 2010](src-66):644, [Martirosyan 2010](src-79):576-578, [EWAia](src-172) II:818, [ALEW](src-352) 1032-1034, [Derksen 2015](src-353):448-449, [Derksen 2008](src-81):485, [de Vaan 2008](src-54):134-135, [Matasović 2009](src-50):223-224). The irregular correspondence of Proto-Indo-Iranic word-initial *j́ʰ- (> Ved. h-, Av. z-) is presumably due to contamination with Proto-Indo-Iranic outcome of PIE *g̑ʰórh₁-/g̑ʰr̥h₁- 'guts', cf. cognate set 2672.
